text: During a laparoscopic distal pancreatectomy for a neuroendocrine tumor, the surgeon mobilizes the short gastric vessels and opens the lesser sac, revealing significant intra-abdominal fat. The pancreas is covered with fat, and the surgeon attempts to identify the splenic vessels posteriorly, with the splenic vein becoming visible.

The surgeon skeletonizes the tissue laterally with the patient in the full decubitus position, left side up, aiding the dissection. The tail of the pancreas is identified, appearing atrophic and distant from the splenic hilum. The abundant intra-abdominal fat complicates the identification of the tumor's precise location.
The surgeon uses an intraoperative laparoscopic ultrasound probe to identify the tumor's location more posteriorly within the pancreas gland, preparing to start skeletonizing the surrounding tissue.

The surgeon exposes the splenic artery and vein, which have been dissected and are visible in the surgical field. Remaining attachments are severed using a harmonic scalpel. The lesion is stapled out once the surgeon is proximal to it. Mild oozing in the area is controlled with Surgicel.
The spleen is visible, showing good perfusion overall, with a small area of ischemia observed at the superior pole.
